## 1. Purpose

This document establishes procedures for classifying, preparing, and entering Confined Spaces to protect the health and safety of workers required to enter them.
blocks.heading_path: Confined Space Entry Procedure > 1. Purpose

blocks.text:
## 2. Definitions

**2.1.** Confined Space – a space on the rig or yard that:

- a. Has limited openings for entry and/or exit
- b. May contain known or potential hazards
- c. Is not intended for continuous occupancy
- d. Has insufficient natural ventilation

*Examples of confined spaces in H&P Drilling Operations include but are not limited to:*
blocks.heading_path: Confined Space Entry Procedure > 2. Definitions > 2.1 Confined Space

blocks.text: Confined spaces are defined as a tank, mud pit, and tunnel or similar where there is a danger of explosion, lack of oxygen or the presence of toxic gases. All spaces that are not normally lit, not normally ventilated and not normally manned or designed to be manned are also considered confined spaces, as are spaces with difficult or restricted access.

blocks.additive_detail: KCAD adds confined-space identification cues not explicit in HP: spaces not normally lit, spaces with difficult or restricted access, and tunnel as an example.
blocks.ai_reasoning: HP Candidate 0 is the closest match because both chunks are informational definition text establishing what qualifies as a confined space before later permit and entry controls apply. The KCAD chunk is a regional Oman variant of the same core concept, adding specific classification cues and examples not visible in the HP definition chunk, but no direct operational contradiction is shown in the provided text.

**2.2.** Acceptable Entry Conditions – the conditions that must exist in a Confined Space to ensure employees can safely enter and work within the space.

**2.3.** Authorized Attendant – an individual posted outside the Confined Space who monitors the person in the space and performs all Attendant’s duties assigned by the Confined Space Entry Procedure

**2.4.** Authorized Entrant – an employee who is authorized by the Entry Supervisor (Local Area Authority) to enter a Confined Space.

**2.5.** Blanking or Blinding – the absolute closure of the pipe, line, or duct by the fastening of a solid plate that completely covers the bore and can withstand the maximum pressure of the pipe, line, or duct with no leakage beyond the plate. Attempts should be made to blind at the closest point to the Confined Spaces.

**2.6.** Double Block and Bleed – the closure of a line or pipe by closing and locking or tagging two in-line valves and by opening and locking or tagging a drain or vent in the line between the two closed valves.

**2.7.** Entry – the action of a person passing through an opening into a Confined Space. Entry occurs as soon as any part of the person’s body breaks the plane of an opening into the space.

**2.8.** Entry Permit – H&Ps written or electronic document that is prepared by the Entry Supervisor (Local Area Authority) authorizing and controlling entry into a Confined Space. Acceptable entry conditions are noted on the document or electronic permit.

**2.9.** Entry Supervisor (Local Area Authority) – the person responsible for:

- a. Determining if acceptable entry conditions are present where entry is planned
- b. Authorizing entry
- c. Terminating entry as required

**2.10.** Hazardous Atmosphere – an atmosphere that may expose employees to risk of death, incapacitation, impairment of the ability to self-rescue, injury, or acute illness from one or more of the following causes:

- a. Atmosphere oxygen concentration below 19.5% (oxygen deficient) or above 23.5% (oxygen enriched).
- b. Flammable gas, vapor, or mist in excess of 10% of the Lower Explosive Limit (LEL).
- c. Airborne combustible dust at a concentration that meets or exceeds its Lower Explosive Limits (LEL).
- d. Any other atmospheric condition that is Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health (IDLH).
- e. Atmospheric concentration of any toxic substance in excess of that substance’s Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL).

**2.11.** Hot Work Permit – H&P’s or Operator’s written or electronic authorization to perform operations which are capable of:

- a. Providing a source of ignition in an area where there is also the potential for a flammable or explosive atmosphere
- b. Creating an oxygen deficient atmosphere while burning welding rods.

**2.12.** Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health (IDLH) – any condition that:

- a. Poses an immediate or delayed threat to life
- b. Would cause irreversible health problems
- c. Would interfere with an individual’s ability to escape from a Confined Space without assistance

**2.13.** Inerting – displacing an explosive atmosphere in a Confined Space with a Non-combustible gas such as nitrogen to produce a Non-combustible atmosphere. This procedure also produces an IDLH oxygen deficient atmosphere.

**2.14.** Isolation – the process of removing a Confined Space from service and completely protecting anyone who may enter from the release of energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, steam) or hazardous chemicals into the space.

**2.15.** Mechanical Lifting Device – any tool or piece of equipment that may be used to retrieve an authorized entrant from a Confined Space. Examples include tripods, hoist, snatch block, come-along, chain falls.

**2.16.** Oxygen Deficient Atmosphere – an atmosphere containing less than 19.5% oxygen by volume in the air. It is not cable of sustaining life.

**2.17.** Oxygen Enriched Atmosphere – an atmosphere containing more than 23.5% oxygen by volume in the air. It is not cable of sustaining life.

**2.18.** Permit System – H&P’s or the Operator’s written procedure for preparing and issuing permits for entry and returning Confined Space to service following completion of the work.

**2.19.** Prohibited Condition – condition in a Confined Space that is not allowed by the Permit during the period when entry is authorized.

**2.20.** Rescue Service – the person designated to rescue employees from Confined Space. This does not include the Authorized Attendant.

**2.21.** Retrieval System – the equipment used for non-entry rescue of person from a Confined Space. Components include a retrieval line, full body harness, and a mechanical lifting device.

**2.22.** Testing – the process of identifying and evaluating the atmospheric hazards that may harm personnel who go into a Confined Space using a properly calibrated gas detector. Test results are recorded on the Entry Permit.

blocks.text:
## 3. DEFINITIONS, ABBREVIATIONS AND ACRONYMS

### ANSI

American National Standards Institute

### Authorised Gas Tester

A person that has received a specific training for the proper use of gas tester and deemed competent to use it.

### CHB

Chemical Hazard Bulletin

### CO

Carbon Monoxide

### Confined Space

Any enclosed space that has the following characteristics:

- It is large enough or so configured that an employee can bodily enter and perform assigned work.
- It is limited or has restricted means for entry or exit.
- It is not designed for continuous employee occupancy.

Confined space usually has limited ventilation and might contain atmosphere that could be dangerous or incompatible due to toxic gases, Oxygen deficiency / enrichment or other contaminants.

Examples of confined spaces (not exhaustive) in an onshore drilling rig:

- Mud tank.
- Chemical vessel
- Cellar

### Confined Space Entry Certificate

The certificate issued for any type of Confined Space Entry.

### Gas Detector

Portable equipment utilized capable to carry out atmosphere checks (i.e., hydrocarbon gas, oxygen, toxic components)

### HSSET

Health, Safety, Security, Environment and Training

### Isolation

The separation of a confined space from unwanted forms of energy which could be a serious hazard to entrants. Isolation is usually accomplished by such means as blanking or blinding; removal or misalignment of pipe sections or double block and bleed; or lockout/tag-out.

### JSA

Job Safety Analysis

### LFL

Lower Flammable Limit - the minimum concentration of a combustible/flammable gas or vapour in air that is necessary to support its combustion. Below this level the mixture is too ‘lean’ to burn.

### Oxygen Deficient Atmosphere

Any atmosphere with Oxygen concentration of 19.5% or less at normal atmospheric pressure.

### Oxygen Enriched Atmosphere

Any atmosphere with an oxygen concentration greater than 23.5% at normal atmospheric pressure.

### PPE

Personal Protective Equipment

### RPE

Respiratory Protective Equipment

### SCBA

Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus

### SDS

Safety Data Sheet

### Standby Man

An individual trained in attendant responsibilities, confined space hazards, and emergency procedures, assigned to remain outside the entry point of the confined space to guard against unauthorised entry, maintain communication with and monitor the authorised entrants inside.

### TRIC

Toolbox Talk Risk Identification Card.

### UFL

Upper Flammable Limit - the maximum concentration of a combustible/flammable gas or vapour in air that is necessary to support its combustion. Above this the mixture is too ‘rich’ to burn.

### Work Permit

These are formal documents specifying work to be done and precautions to be taken. Work can only start when safe procedures have been defined and put into place. The permit provides a clear written record, signed by a responsible person, that all foreseeable hazards have been considered and all necessary actions have been taken. It must be in the possession of the person in charge of the operation before work can commence.

blocks.text:
## 3. Process

**3.1.** Atmospheric testing will be conducted prior to and continuously during the Confined Space Entry. Toxic gases may not be detectable until sludge or scale is disturbed in the Confined Space. The initial results of testing will be recorded on the electronic or written permit. If during continuous monitoring conditions become immediately dangerous, the job will be stopped, the permit will be closed, and the supervisor notified.
blocks.heading_path: Confined Space Entry Procedure > 3. Process > 3.1

blocks.text:
### AUTHORIZED GAS TESTER:

The person performing the gas testing and/or air quality sampling prior to entry must have undergone applicable training in the gas testing process. The air quality will be tested and recorded on the PTW issued for the confined space entry job. The atmosphere inside the space must be continuously monitored while work is being carried out. If the confined space is left for any reason, the atmosphere shall be re-tested before re-entry may be permitted. The atmosphere shall be retested after any emergency case or situation.

The authorised gas tester should also:

- Understand the function and limitations of the test equipment in use.
- Prove the safe atmosphere (i.e. free of toxic and flammable gases) in the confined space using correctly calibrated gas testing equipment.
- Prove oxygen, flammable and toxic levels within acceptable limits
- Enter the results obtained while testing the atmosphere onto the CSE permit.

## 3. Process

**3.1.** Atmospheric testing will be conducted prior to and continuously during the Confined Space Entry. Toxic gases may not be detectable until sludge or scale is disturbed in the Confined Space. The initial results of testing will be recorded on the electronic or written permit. blocks.text:
### 3.9 VERIFYING AIR QUALITY

For all confined space entry, sounding must be done before entering. In case of a doubtful sounding or suspected presence of fluid that presents a drowning hazard, all fluid must be removed before any person is permitted to enter the confined space.

An adequate system of communication must be agreed upon and tested by all involved to ensure that those entering the space can keep in touch with the stand-by person stationed at the entrance. The time of opening or closing a confined space and entry or exit of personnel must be recorded at the manned control point. (i.e. on the control board at the worksite and by informing the radio operator or HSEA).

##### Testing the Atmosphere

95% of confined space fatalities have occurred because the atmosphere inside the space was not tested before entry.

##### Why We Need Gas Detection

We normally breathe in:

- Nitrogen 78.09 %
- Oxygen 20.95 %
- Carbon dioxide 0.04 %
- Other gases 0.92 %

Poisonous and toxic gases can alter the composition of air, affecting the exchange of O₂ and CO₂ in the lungs, cells and blood.

This may then affect the brain and alter our breathing rate and frequency.

It also protects your workplace against oxygen fire and explosion

##### Symptoms of Oxygen Deficiency

- **18% – 14% Vol.** Increased breathing volume accelerated heartbeat Impaired attention, thinking and coordination
- **14% – 10% Vol.** Faulty judgment, Rapid fatigue, Heart damage, intermittent respiration
- **10% – 6% Vol.** Nausea and vomiting, Vigorous movement, Unconsciousness, death
- **Less than 6% Vol.** Spasmodic breathing, Death in minutes

##### Measuring Strategies You Should Know

Test at each position for at least 1 minute.

Wait an additional 30 seconds for samples to travel through a hose probe (if used) to the detector. Irregular confined space shapes may trap gases; make sure you analyze the entire atmosphere.

Know the special features of the detector.

Ventilate all confined areas completely with normal air, using recommended procedures even if no hazard was detected by testing.

Initial testing should be done by a person designated as “competent” by having the proper training and experience to perform air quality testing on the rig.

It is important to understand that some gases or vapors are heavier than air and will settle to the bottom of a confined space. Also, some gases are lighter than air and will be found around the top of the confined space. Therefore, it is necessary to test all areas (top, middle, and bottom) of a confined space with properly calibrated testing instruments to determine what gases are present. Atmospheres may be different in individual bays of the same tank.

If testing reveals oxygen-deficiency, or the presence of toxic gases or vapors, the space must be ventilated and re-tested before workers enter. If ventilation is not possible and entry is necessary (for emergency rescue, for example), workers must have appropriate respiratory protection.

**NEVER TRUST YOUR SENSES TO DETERMINE IF THE AIR IN A CONFINED SPACE IS SAFE! YOU CAN NOT SEE OR SMELL MANY TOXIC GASES AND VAPORS, NOR CAN YOU DETERMINE THE LEVEL OF OXYGEN PRESENT.**

Testing instruments for oxygen and flammability read in percent. The oxygen reading should indicate 20.9 % Oxygen in the space being tested. The flammability indicator shows the percent within a safety range of 0-10% of the Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) and, ideally, should read 0%.

Toxins are measured in parts per million (PPM) sometimes in a glass tube that changes color to indicate the PPM content of the space. A different type of tube must be used for each toxin suspected to be present.

Testing instruments are available in several different forms, hand powered by squeezing a rubber bulb or bellows, and battery powered giving the indication either on an analog gauge or digital readout.

Testing instruments must be calibrated frequently to assure accuracy.

The atmosphere must be continuously monitored with a portable gas detector to verify that the atmosphere remains gas free and that an acceptable oxygen level is maintained.

Air movers or blowers must be used for venting and to provide a continuous supply of fresh air while the work is in progress, unless sufficient airflow is obtained through a free flow process. Air movers or blowers must be installed in such a position that the fresh air intakes do not draw in exhausted fumes or vapors.

Provisions must be made for ready access and egress. Tanks, vessels or other confined spaces having openings or manholes in the side as well as in the top, must be entered from the side openings or manholes when practicable.

The use of a safety line to indicate the direct route to and from the worker must be considered in the situation of a side entry.

Fuel and crude oil tanks must not be entered without rig manager/drilling superintendent approval.

No source of ignition may be introduced into a confined space where flammable vapours or gases may be present.

When working in confined spaces all pipelines discharging into that space must be closed with blind flanges, plugs or valves and energy isolation signs and tags posted. Isolation by means of valve(s) should be checked and proven prior to any entry.

There must be at least two rescue team personnel in the confined space and one rescue team person outside the confined space equipped with approved respiratory equipment for all rescue situations in a confined space requiring respiratory equipment.

### Toxic Gases and Fumes

By nature toxic gases are harmful and in sufficient concentrations will cause irritations and in some cases, brain damage and death.

Certain gases whilst not having directly harmful effects on the lungs can affect the circulatory system and internal organs.

Gas detectors come in many different shapes, sizes and sensor configurations but they have many similarities. Most have a screen with digital readout or a meter with a needle and scale. The type of detector used should be a multi head type detector

There are also operating and menu buttons, a buzzer or speaker, strokes, sensors and attachment points on the device.

#### Care of the Detector

- It is best to keep the unit on charge whenever it isn’t being used but try and discharge the battery completely once a month to ensure good battery life.
- Read the operating manual for product specific maintenance intervals and determine what care is needed and during use, pre-use & post use. Every six months send in for re-certification. Every recertification shall be kept in a register maintained at the rig site. Gas detectors should be kept charged at all times with back up batteries or chargers available.

blocks.text:
**3.2.** H&P employees shall not enter a Confined Space that has any of the following conditions:

- a. Contains or has the potential to contain a hazardous explosive or toxic atmosphere
- b. Contains an oxygen deficient or oxygen enriched atmosphere
- c. Contains a material that has the potential for engulfing the person who enters
- d. Has an internal configuration such that a person who enters could be trapped or asphyxiated by inwardly converging walls or by a floor that slopes downward and tapers down to a smaller cross section
- e. Contains any recognized serious safety or health hazard

**3.3.** If all hazards cannot be mitigated, the Confined Space shall not be entered

**3.4.** Employees in a Confined Space will have a means of retrieval attached to them while in the Confined Space and an Authorized Attendant will be stationed at the entry to the Confined Space responsible for operating the retrieval system.
blocks.heading_path: Confined Space Entry Procedure > 3. Process > 3.2-3.4

blocks.text:
### 5.9.FIRE AND EXPLOSION

Only Explosion Proof, Reduced Low-Voltage Electric Portable Tools and Lights shall be used inside the Confined Space.

Inside the tank, welding and flame cutting at the same time are not allowed.

NOTE:

- No activities in confined space are allowed without SCBA, if the gas detector's reading is equal to or greater than 10% LEL.
- Hot Work must not be undertaken if the gas detectors’ reading exceeds 1% LEL.

blocks.text:
### 3.11 HOT WORK

In addition to the above, as per KCADeutag PTW procedure KCAD-LAND-PTWP-001, the following hot work procedures must also be adhered to:

- If a high level of combustible gases or oxygen enriched atmosphere (above 20.9%) is detected, the area must be naturally aired or ventilated and a new test carried out.
- Repeat the procedure until the test indicates the area is safe for hot work.
- If torch cutting or welding is carried out on pipelines passing through confined spaces, they must be isolated, purged if necessary, energy isolation signs and tags posted prior to the hot work starting.
- One of the highest risks from using gas within a confined space is the accumulation of gas due to leakage from the cylinders or hoses. To reduce this risk, gas cylinders should not be taken into confined spaces. Where it is necessary to take gas cylinders into confined spaces, a specific gas check must be made in the vicinity of the cylinders prior to recommencing work after a break. The cylinders must be removed immediately on completion of the task.
- When hoses are run from gas cylinders outside the confined space, all hoses and fittings must be disconnected from the gas cylinders or removed from the confined space during extended breaks.

**3.2.** H&P employees shall not enter a Confined Space that has any of the following conditions:

- a. Contains or has the potential to contain a hazardous explosive or toxic atmosphere
- b. Contains an oxygen deficient or oxygen enriched atmosphere
- c. Contains a material that has the potential for engulfing the person who enters
- d. Has an internal configuration such that a person who enters could be trapped or asphyxiated by inwardly converging walls or by a floor that slopes downward and tapers down to a smaller cross section
- e. Contains any recognized serious safety or health hazard

**3.3.** If all hazards cannot be mitigated, the Confined Space shall not be entered

**3.4.** Employees in a Confined Space will have a means of retrieval attached to them while in the Confined Space and an Authorized Attendant will be stationed at the entry to the Confined Space responsible for operating the retrieval system.

### 5.4.ENVIRONMENTAL CONDITIONS CONSIDERATION

Environmental conditions such as ambient temperature and relative humidity in air shall be addressed in JSA / Risk Assessment. Confined space entry jobs will be planned with consideration of heat index (combination of ambient temperature and relative humidity) parameter, in periods of day with the lower heat index measure, as far as reasonably practicable.

When confined space entry during high heat of relative humidity levels cannot be avoided, adequate mitigating measures shall be planned implemented.

## 5. PROCEDURE

### 5.1.GENERAL

#### HIERARCHY OF CONTROLS F FOR ENTRY INTO CONFINED SPACE

Entry to Confined Space control measures should be implemented in the workplace in priority order. Consequently, complete elimination of the need to enter the confined spaced should be the first consideration in controlling risk to workers. It is only if this is not practicable that consideration should be given to the second measure in the hierarchy:

- Elimination.
- Substitution.
- Isolation.
- Engineering controls.
- Adoption of safe work practices.
- Use of personal protective equipment, – refer to section 5.3.4

blocks.text:
### 3.2 GENERAL/PHYSICAL HAZARDS

In addition to the areas discussed above, evaluation of a confined space should consider the following potential hazards:

**1. Temperature Extremes**  
Extremely hot or cold temperatures can present problems for workers. For example, if the space has been steamed, it should be allowed to cool before any entry is made.

**2. Engulfment Hazards**  
Loose, granular material stored in hoppers such as bulk cement, bulk barite, and bulk mud can engulf and suffocate a worker. The loose material can break loose from the sides of the hoppers or bulk drilling mud can enter from another tank, and in the process of this happening, the worker can be engulfed and suffocate.

**3. Noise**  
Noise within a confined space can be amplified by the design and acoustic properties of the space. Excessive noise can not only damage hearing, but can also affect communication, such as causing a shouted warning to go unheard.

**4. Slick / Wet Surfaces**  
Slips and falls can occur on a wet surface causing injury or death to workers. Also, a wet surface will increase the likelihood for and effect of electric shock in areas where electrical circuits, equipment, and tools are used.

**5. Falling Objects**  
Workers in confined spaces should be mindful of the possibility of falling objects, particularly in spaces which have topside opening for entry, and where work is being done above the worker.

**6. Hazardous Atmospheres**  
As mentioned, the atmosphere in a confined space may be extremely hazardous because of the lack of natural air movement. This characteristic of confined spaces can result in 1) oxygen-deficient atmospheres, 2) flammable atmospheres, and/or 3) toxic atmospheres.

##### OXYGEN-DEFICIENT ATMOSPHERES:

An oxygen-deficient atmosphere has less than 19.5% available oxygen (O₂). Any atmosphere with less than 19.5% oxygen should not be entered! The oxygen level in a confined space can decrease because of work being done, such as welding, cutting, or brazing; or, it can be decreased by certain chemical reactions (rusting, paint drying) or through bacterial action (fermentation). The oxygen level is also decreased if oxygen is displaced by another gas, such as carbon dioxide or nitrogen, resulting from inerting or fire suppression. Total displacement of oxygen by another gas, such as carbon dioxide, will result in unconsciousness, followed by death.

##### FLAMMABLE ATMOSPHERES

Two things make an atmosphere flammable: 1) the oxygen in air, and 2) a flammable gas, vapor, or dust in the proper mixture. Different gases have different flammable ranges. If a source of ignition (e.g., a sparking or electrical tool, static electricity, sand blasting) is introduced into a space containing a flammable atmosphere, an explosion will result. An oxygen-enriched atmosphere (above 23.5%) will cause flammable materials, such as clothing and hair, to burn violently when ignited. Therefore, never use pure oxygen to ventilate a confined space. Ventilate with normal air taken from a known, clean source.

##### TOXIC ATMOSPHERES

Unless the space has been tested and deemed safe, assume that any substance (liquids, vapors, gases, mists, solid materials, and dusts) in a confined space can be hazardous. Toxic substances may range from fast acting poisons to long term cancer causing carcinogens.

Toxic substances can come from the following:

**The product stored in the space:**  
The product can be absorbed into the structure and give off toxic gases when removed or when cleaning out the residue of a stored product, toxic gases can be given off. Example: Removal of sludge or mud from a tank – decomposed material can give off deadly hydrogen sulfide gas.

**The work being performed in a confined space:**  
Examples of such include welding, cutting, brazing, painting, scraping, sand blasting, degreasing, etc. Toxic atmospheres are generated in various processes. For example, cleaning solvents are used in many industries for cleaning/degreasing. The vapors from these solvents are very toxic in a confined space.

**Areas adjacent to the confined space:**  
Toxicants produced by work in the area of the confined spaces can enter and accumulate in confined spaces.

**Pyrophoric substances/scale:**  
Pyrophoric scale is formed when the sulphur in H₂S reacts with rusted metal surfaces in atmospheres with little or no oxygen, leaving iron sulfide deposits. During tank cleaning, when sludge or other solids deposited over them are disturbed, these deposits will oxidize rapidly when exposed to air and can produce sufficient heat to auto-ignite or ignite any hydrocarbon-air mixture present within the vessel.

### 3.0 PROCEDURES

### CONFINED SPACE ENTRY

### 3.1 REGISTER OF CONFINED SPACES

All confined space entry points must be numbered and stencilled or sign provided stating “PTW REQUIRED BEFORE ENTRY”. A log book or register must be maintained by the HSEA to identify all confined space entry points and for cross referencing the confined space on the TRIC card. ## Scope:

Any persons on a KCADeutag site entering confined spaces will be trained in the hazards of confined space entry. This training includes:

- The known risks to personnel created by a confined space
- Precautions to address the risks
- Control measures, their purpose and how to use them
- Use of necessary PPE
- Emergency Procedures
- Training on understanding and application of KCAD CSE procedure.

### 3.10 ISOLATIONS

As per KCAD lock out – tag out (LOTO) procedure KCAD-Land-LOTO-001, isolation of a confined space is a process where the space is removed from service by:

- Locking out electrical sources, preferably at disconnect switches remote from the equipment.
- Blanking and bleeding, securing valves, cargo, ballast, pneumatic and hydraulic lines.
- Disconnecting mechanical linkages on shaft-driven equipment where possible, and securing mechanical moving parts within confined spaces with latches, chains, chocks, blocks, or other devices.
- Type of isolation e.g. LOTO, double block and bleed, spades or blind flanges (positive isolation) should be specified in the PTW.

### 5.5.ISOLATION

Before entering the confined space, it could be also required to provide adequate isolation (mechanical, electrical) along with the Lock-out and Tag-out. The isolation shall be done by competent person duly authorised by a dedicated work permit.

Method of isolation shall be selected from the following:

- Physical disconnection.
- Removal of a valve, spool piece, an expansion joint in piping leading to, and as close as possible to, the confined space and blanking or capping the open end of the piping leading to the confined space.
- Insertion of a suitable full-pressure spade (blank) in piping between the flanges nearest to the confined space.
- Where neither method above is practicable, isolation by means of closing and locking, or closing and tagging, or both, of at least two valves in the pipe leading to the confined space shall suffice.
- Before entry is permitted to any confined space which itself can move, or in which agitators, fans or other moving parts which may pose a risk to personnel are present, movement shall be prevented by an approved means or an alternative method offering the equivalent security.

The relevant (mechanical/electrical) isolations required for the task will be identified in JSA and/or Risk Assessment and will be carried out by the authorised person mechanical or electrical.

The isolations shall be achieved in accordance with the Isolations requirements (refer to Work Permit System Procedure K-KS-HS-PR-008) and shall be verified by the Workgroup Leader.

blocks.text:
### 5.10. CHEMICAL/TOXIC SUBSTANCES

All substances, which are likely to present a hazard to persons inside the confined space, should be removed as far as possible prior to entry. Potentially dangerous material may be trapped in sludge, scale, or other deposits, behind loose linings, in liquid traps, or in instrument fittings, and may be released only when for example, it is disturbed, or heat is applied. Similarly, such material may lodge in joints in vessels or in the bends of connecting pipes, or other places where removal is difficult. Procedures and processes to be used to clean the inside of a confined space should be reviewed and authorised prior to entry.

Contaminants should be disposed of in a manner that will not constitute a hazard.

Whenever practicable, initial cleaning should be performed from outside the confined space. Such initial cleaning should continue until the hazard of atmospheric contaminants has been reduced as far as reasonably practicable possible.

### 5.10. CHEMICAL/TOXIC SUBSTANCES

Chemical substances can be toxic causing injury, acute or chronic illness or death, depending on the concentration, duration of exposure and the characteristics of the substances.

A toxic substance can cause harm by inhalation, ingestion, skin, or eye contact. It can affect the tissue at the point of contact, or organs remote from the point of contact. Typical toxic and caustic substances present in confined space in drilling activities are derived from use and storage of mud.

The method to be used will depend upon the material in the confined space and the potential hazards that may be created by the cleaning process itself.

blocks.text:
### 3.8 VENTILATION

Before completely removing the fastening devices on a confined space, the internal pressure must be checked and vented if necessary.

The atmosphere must be sampled for oxygen levels and combustible gas using a portable gas detector by a qualified gas tester. If levels of toxic, inert, or combustible gases or oxygen levels are detected that present dangerous or hazardous conditions, the area must not be entered until measures have been taken to render the space safe. If any work is to take place inside vessels which have or which may have contained combustible or hydrocarbon liquids, those vessels shall be drained and flushed to ensure removal of all such contaminants and purged to provide safe atmosphere prior to entry. Under no circumstances will entry be permitted into spaces containing flammable or combustible liquids or atmospheres.

All equipment used for testing purposes must be maintained and calibrated.

Only persons who are competent should carry out gas testing. Those conducting the gas testing should also be capable of interpreting the results and taking any necessary action.

Testing must be conducted for each re-entry.

There are substantial risks if the concentration of oxygen in the atmosphere varies significantly from normal, 20.9 %. For example, oxygen enrichment will increase flammability of clothing and other combustible materials, oxygen deficiency will induce impairment to personnel.

Atmosphere below 19.5% or above 23.5% oxygen by volume must not be entered except for emergency rescue purposes and then only when personnel are equipped with positive pressure respirators.

Ventilation by a blower or fan may be necessary to remove harmful gases and vapors from a confined space. There are several methods for ventilating a confined space. The method and equipment chosen are dependent upon the size of the confined space openings, the gases to be exhausted (e.g., are they flammable or heavier than air?), and the source of makeup air.

Under certain conditions where flammable gases or vapors have displaced the oxygen level, but are too rich to burn, forced air ventilation may dilute them until they are within the explosive range.

Also, if inert gases (e.g. carbon dioxide, nitrogen) are used in the confined space, the space should be well ventilated and re-tested before a worker may enter.

A common method of ventilation requires a large hose, one end attached to a fan and the other lowered into a manhole or opening. For example, a manhole would have the ventilating hose run to the bottom to exhaust all harmful gases and vapors. An air intake should be placed in an area that will draw in fresh air only.

Ventilation should be continuous where possible, because in many confined spaces the hazardous atmosphere will form again when the flow of air is stopped.

### 5.6.VENTILATION

To disperse traces of hazardous substances and to ensure that atmosphere has a sufficient oxygen level, an adequate ventilation system must be in place.

Where it is necessary to use air movers to ensure that the confined space atmosphere is maintained in a habitable state, the following criteria must be followed:

- The source of air for the mover must always draw from an uncontaminated source i.e., from outside the area. Where hot work is to take place inside the confined space, care must be taken not to create a blast furnace effect by using both air pushers and pullers at the same time. In this type of situation only one or the other shall be used.

blocks.text:
### 5.9.FIRE AND EXPLOSION

Fires and explosion can result from accumulations of flammable vapours and/or dust in a present of source of ignition.

Mixture of flammable vapours and air can only be ignited if the hydrocarbon to air ratio is within the flammable range i.e. between the LEL and the UEL.

Explosive/flammable mixtures may develop typically during the emptying of vessel or tank and the opening of confined space due to air entering and mixing with the residual gases.

A source of ignition can be any heat source having enough energy to ignite a flammable gas/air mixture or to raise the temperature above the auto-ignition temperature. In addition to naked flames, other sources of ignition include:

- Sparks or arcs produced by electrical equipment, lightning, and electrostatic charges.
- Heat of friction during drilling or other non-flame cutting operation.
- Pyrophoric material (which could burn in air or by friction with another material).

### 5.8.DEFICIENCY OR EXCESS OF OXYGEN

Storage cylinders, gas hoses, and valves must be handled with care and should be daily inspected for damage.

Gas Cylinder must not be taken into confined spaces or gas allowed to migrate to them. Cutting and welding equipment must always be removed from confined spaces during breaks and at the end of the working shift.

NOTE:

- With level of oxygen between the parameters indicated in section 5.7, operation can undergo without using SCBA Protection with all other precaution in place as per Work Permit requirement.
- If the level of oxygen is steady between 16% of 20% and it is not possible to increase, extra protection is required, and operation can undergo under the approval of Toolpusher/Night pusher using 30-min SCBA.

blocks.text:
### 5.8.DEFICIENCY OR EXCESS OF OXYGEN

Oxygen deficiency in air results in asphyxiation. Atmospheres containing less than 19.5% are oxygen deficient.

Main causes which may result in oxygen deficiency:

- Ventilation system failure / poor full air exchange rate.
- Oxidation of metal (rusting).

Main causes which may result in oxygen enrichment:

- Leaks from Oxygen containing equipment.
- Inadvertent use of oxygen instead of air or inert gas.
- Deliberate addition of oxygen.

The most common oxygen-containing equipment is that used in cutting operations.

#### Confined Space Entry Checklist

| Item | Value/Check |
|---|---|
| Personnel entering confined space have been trained in the hazards of confined space entry. |  |
| Approved Permit to Work has been obtained. |  |
| Designated trained stand-by person assigned to standby the confined space entrance at all times. |  |
| Oxygen / Gas detector is present and is calibrated. |  |
| Minimum of two explosion-proof portable lights in use. |  |
| Explosion-proof walkie talkies in use. |  |
| Appropriate warning signs / barricades in use. |  |
| Portable tripod with a combined fall arrestor-retrieving winch or similar system in use. |  |
| A full body safety harness to be worn while working inside the confined space. |  |
| Internal pressure checked and vented before removing fastening devices on confined space. |  |
| Designated standby person will monitor air quality upon entry and each re-entry. |  |
| Oxygen level is between 19.5% to 22%. |  |
| Do Not Enter if it is above or below this range! |  |
| Air quality tested for H2S / Explosive gases – None present. |  |
| Confined space will be sounded for fluid before being entered. Flotation device will be worn if a drowning hazard exists. |  |
| Standby person will maintain constant radio contact with persons in confined space and control room. (Radio Room) |  |
| Standby person knows how to raise the alarm if persons inside confined space require emergency assistance and knows not to enter confined space until assistance arrives. |  |
| Adequate rescue equipment is readily available and standby person is familiar with its use. |  |
| Standby Person will keep a tally of number / names of persons inside confined space. |  |
| Standby person will notify Person In Charge for a relief watchman to be assigned as relief and wait until being properly relieved before leaving the post. |  |
| Adequate handover and safety briefing will be conducted with any person who relieves the standby person or crew members working in the confined space. |  |
| Explosion proof ventilation will be used for a continuous supply of fresh air unless sufficient airflow is obtained through a free flow process. |  |
| No source of ignition will be introduced into a confined space where flammable vapors or gases may be present. |  |
| All pipelines discharging into that space will be closed with blind flanges, plugs or valves and energy isolation signs and tags posted. |  |
| If torch cutting or welding is carried out on pipelines passing through confined spaces, they will be isolated, purged if necessary, energy isolation signs and tags posted prior to the hot work starting. |  |
| Oxygen / Acetylene hoses will be removed from confined space during extended breaks and air re-tested for gas before re-entry. |  |
| The time of opening or closing of confined space and entry or exit of personnel will be recorded at the manned control point (Radio Room). |  |
| Person in charge: ____________________ (Name) ____________________ (Sign) |  |
| Date: ____________________ Time: ____________________ |  |
| Standby Person: ____________________ (Name) ____________________ (Sign) |  |

### Exposure Limits - Calculations

|  |  |
|---|---|
| T W A | **Time Weighted Average.** How much can you be exposed to during a normal working week, in Parts Per Million (p.p.m.), the same as 0.0001%. |
| S T E L | **Short-Term Exposure Limit.** How much, in p.p.m., can you be exposed to for up to 15 minutes, 4 times a day, with an hour break in between exposures. |
| I D L H | **Immediately Dangerous to Life Hazard.** How much, in p.p.m., will create an immediate threat to your health or life. |

## 7. Permit Preparation

blocks.text:
### PERSON IN CHARGE OF WORK (WORKSITE SUPERVISOR):

The worksite supervisor or person in charge of work for the planned activity is responsible for ensuring that the necessary documentation i.e. PTW, work instructions, JSA/TRA, checklist(s) and TRIC are all correctly completed in place. ### 2.0 RESPONSABILITIES

### PERSON IN CHARGE (PIC):

**All other options must be considered before CSE is to be implemented**

The Person in Charge (PIC) is responsible for ensuring that the entrant(s) and the standby man are all trained and competent to perform the task and fully understand their roles and responsibilities as related to the task. The PIC will also define controls and measures to be observed throughout the task including frequency of air quality sampling & gas testing of the atmosphere inside the space.

The PIC shall inform the area supervisor of the planned work in the confined space to ensure he is aware of the forthcoming work in his area to prevent the possibility of SIMOPS arising or any other conflicts of work.

## 7. Permit Preparation

The Authorized Attendant is responsible for preparing the Confined Space Permit.

blocks.text: **7.1.** Atmospheric testing will be conducted prior to and continuously during the Confined Space Entry. Toxic gases may not be detectable until sludge or scale is disturbed in the Confined Space. The initial results of testing will be recorded on the electron or written permit. If during continuous monitoring conditions become immediately dangerous the job will be stopped, the permit will be closed, and the supervisor notified.

blocks.text:
### 5.7.ATMOSPHERIC TEST

Before entry, it is necessary to test the atmosphere in the confined space for oxygen levels, flammability, toxicity and/or any contaminants that have a potential to be present in that confined space to ensure a safe working environment.

This testing must be done by an Authorised Gas Tester using adequate equipment, properly certified and calibrated.

Testing of the confined spaces shall be conducted throughout the entire portion of the space that workers will occupy during the entry.

The ventilation system must be shut down before starting the testing.

Atmospheric conditions for a safe entry are:

- Oxygen level between 20% and 23.5%.
- Flammable/explosive gases not more than 0% LFL/LEL.
- H2S concentration is not more than 0 ppm.
- CO concentration is not more than 0 ppm.
- Other toxic substances are not more than 0%.

If test results conclude that the atmospheric condition of the confined space is unacceptable, entry is prohibited until such conditions are brought into acceptable limits.

If the workers leave the confined space for any significant period, such as for a lunch or other break, the atmosphere of the confined space must be retested before the return of workers in the confined space.

### 5.7.ATMOSPHERIC TEST

Where the entry point is vertical into the confined space, it is recommended that remote probes be used to measure the atmosphere at various levels. This is necessary because some gases and vapours are lighter or heavier than air and can accumulate at various levels in the confined space.

Certain work performed in a confined space can cause the atmosphere in the space to change. Examples of this are welding, drilling, or sludge removal. In these situations, air monitoring of the confined space should be conducted on a continuous basis throughout the time of the entry.

In case the entry point of the confined space is surrounded by live equipment (i.e. pumps, exchanger, etc.), it could be recommended to also perform the gas testing on the external area to avoid the contamination of the confined space.

### 3.7 CONFINED SPACE ENTRY SAFETY CHECKLIST

A confined space entry checklist must be completed at the “pre job phase” and referenced on the TRIC card. (See Appendix 4.1 for an example checklist)

### 5.2.PRE-ENTRY REQUIREMENTS

Before entering a confined space a Work Permit, supplemented by entry certificate, must be completed and all controls identified in JSA and/or Risk Assessment must be in place.

The following hazards shall be considered and safeguarded against:

- Deficiency or excess of Oxygen.
- Fire, explosion.
- Chemical / Toxic Substances.
- Electric shock.
- Dust, particulates.
- Lighting level.
- Static Electricity is caused by steam / water jetting / grit blasting.

blocks.text:
### 3.6 RISK ASSESSMENT (JSA)

Each confined space may be different by design, floor plan, internal pipe work, agitator location etc. Because of this, each confined space **must** be assessed on their individual basis for risk of harm to people and to ensure the following are identified, considered and controlled:

- Slip trip and fall hazards.
- Lighting.
- Condition of ladders.
- Fall protection for access/exit of the confined space.
- Internal pipe work (potential for ingress of fluid).
- Isolation of all machinery (i.e.: mud pit agitator blades, for example).
- The potential for gas release from mud sediment.
- Consider that the opening of the confined space is wide enough to permit a man with a SCBA (Self Contained Breather Apparatus) to enter.
- In the situation the confined space is long, to consider fabrication and fitting of a secondary escape/rescue hatch.
- Simultaneous operations.

### 5.3.RISK ASSESSMENT

Before carrying out work which involves entry into a confined space, a JSA and/or Risk Assessment is necessary to determine what measures need to be taken to ensure the safety and health of those entering and working in the confined space. This JSA will identify the relevant hazards and formulate a safe system of work into the confined space. It will evaluate risks associated with the identified hazards and if the risk is found to be unacceptable, then entry must not be undertaken.

### 5.11. ELECTRIC SHOCK

The use of electrical portable tools, including lights, with a source greater than 50 Volts is prohibited due to electrical shock hazard. If it is necessary to use portable tools with source greater than 50 Volts, isolation transformer shall be installed.

Electrical portable tools, including lights, used inside confined spaces shall be operated on voltage as low as this is reasonably practicable.

Electric shock hazards and mitigating measures shall be identified in the relevant JSA / Risk Assessment for the specific task carried out in the confined space.

### 3.13 PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT (PPE)

PPE is traditionally regarded as the last line of protection with the emphasis being place on avoidance and appropriate managerial control methods. Each confined space will present different hazards and degrees of risk to health and safety, the final provision of PPE should therefore be based on an assessment of risk. Standard PPE must be worn as a minimum. Specialized PPE e.g. welders mask or SCBA may be required.

**As a general rule the following guidance is offered.**

Basic PPE should include:

- Body protection (full body coveralls with suitable pockets for notebook etc).
- Foot protection (steel toecaps (200 joules), steel midsoles, good grip, oil resistant).
- Head protection (hard hat with chinstrap).
- Hand protection (hard wearing gloves).
- Eye protection (protective glasses, goggles).
- Ear protection (ear defenders or ear plugs - worn subject to communication system).
- Lighting (hand held torch with lanyard and appropriate beam width).
- RPE: Dependent upon the type and concentration of contaminants, respiratory protection may range from a simple cartridge respirator to air-supplied breathing apparatus. When the level of airborne contaminants is beyond the filtration capability of a respirator, as defined by the vendor, or where there is an oxygen deficiency, then breathing apparatus must be used.

### 5.14. PROTECTIVE AND EMERGENCY EQUIPMENT

For all works that involve entry into a vessel or confined space, the following minimum protective equipment shall be in place:

- Portable multi-gas detector for gas monitoring.
- Safety harness complete with shoulder D-rings.
- Tripod.
- Safety retrieval rope.
- Respiratory protective equipment (SCBA), where necessary

The rig shall have adequate, fit for purpose confined space rescue equipment which shall meet the requirements of ANSI standards or other equivalent standards.

For additional details of confined space rescue/emergency equipment and equipment inspections (refer to Drilling Safety Equipment Procedure K-KS-HS-PR-004 and Safety to Work at Height Procedure K-KS-HS-PR-032).

### 6.3.EMERGENCY RESCUE EQUIPMENT

The following precautions shall be considered when selecting rescue equipment.

- Equipment to be used for rescue operation must be readily available in the confined space entry point, have proper inspection and certification and must be checked visually prior to be used.
- Tripod winch line is adequately spooled/unspooled, free from knots or stuck.
- Be sure that Assistant Drillers and Derrickmen are designated First Aiders.
- Stairways, walkways, access, and egress shall be always kept free from obstructions.
- Rig Medic informed of confined space entry tasks.

### 3.12 SAFETY EQUIPMENT REQUIREMENTS:

The following equipment shall be available for applicable confined space entries:

- A portable gas detector capable of continuously monitoring the oxygen content, H₂S content and Lower Explosive Limit (LEL), complete with accessories to allow remote detection
- An explosion proof air exhaust fan or venturi.
- Explosion proof lights (if required)
- Explosion proof radios (if required by risk assessment)
- Appropriate warning signs and barricades
- A portable tripod with combined fall arrestor-retrieving winch or similar system
- One company approved full body harness per person
- One 30 minute SCBA per rescue team member
- A stretcher that allows rescue of an injured person from inside the confined space.
- One 15 minute escape set available per person involved in CSE tasks

## 6. EMERGENCY PREPAREDNESS

### 6.1.CONFINED SPACE RESCUE PROCEDURE

- Activate emergency response procedure.
- Atmospheric test to be performed to check whether Gas/H2S is present in the confined space.
- The member shall don breathing apparatus prior to entering the confined space.
- One member of the rescue team should put on a retrieval type harness, descend into the confined space.
- The member attempts to determine the casualty’s condition.
- Erect Tripod on top of the opening of the confined space and Y-lanyard to be connected to the tripod winch line.
- Tripod winch line to be lowered to inside of the confined space.
- The rescuer should then relay this information to the rescue team leader on the spot and wait for further instruction.
- Attach the Y-lanyard to the casualty’s retrieval harness and secure it.
- If further rescue equipment is needed, it should be supplied to the rescuer.
- Give signal to other members out of the confined space to lift the tripod winch line carefully.
- The rescuer guided the casualty body until they came out from the confined space.
- Stretcher shall be ready outside of the confined space and the casualty to be placed on it once rescued from the confined space.
- Site Medic shall be ready with all necessary first aid equipment.
- Rescue shall be completed within 10 minutes or as soon as possible.

NOTE: A casualty with spinal injuries may first be installed on a spine backboard device prior to being laid into the basket.

blocks.text:
### 3.14 EMERGENCY RESPONSE PLAN (ERP)

Before commencing any confined space work, the ERP personnel (including the medic) must know their role and responsibility in the event that a person is injured and requires rescue from the confined space.

The ERP members are those personnel as listed on the installations Station Bill. All ERP members must be fully trained and capable in conducting a safe and fast rescue without endangering the injured person, themselves or other crew members at the worksite.

The weekly fire and evacuation drills must periodically be used (at least every 90 days) for the sole purpose of exercising the ERP crew (including the medic) in the scenario of a person in need of rescue from a confined space.

**Note:**  
The importance for all ERP members to understand their role and responsibility cannot be underestimated as someone’s life may depend on a safe, fast and efficient rescue. **Over 50% of the workers who die in confined spaces are attempting to rescue other workers.** Rescuers must be trained in and follow established emergency procedures and use appropriate equipment and techniques (lifelines, respiratory protection, standby persons, etc.). Steps for safe rescue should be included in all confined space entry procedures. Rescue should be well planned and drills should be frequently conducted on emergency procedures. Unplanned rescue, such as when someone instinctively rushes in to help a downed co-worker, can easily result in a double fatality or even multiple fatalities if there is more than one would-be rescuer.

**REMEMBER AN UNPLANNED RESCUE WILL PROBABLY BE YOUR LAST!**

5. Raise the man out of the tank.
6. Place the man in the stretcher and secure him.
7. Carry the man to the upwind Muster Point.
8. Medic assesses patient, gives necessary first aid and prepares patient for medevac.

#### EVACUATE THE VICTIM TO HOSPITAL

1. Ambulance driver brings the ambulance to the injured person. If there is an H2S release from the well, driver shall only start the vehicle if safe to do so. Driver shall not drive across wind from the well.
2. Rescue team carries the man to the ambulance.
3. Secure the stretcher in the ambulance under the supervision of the Rig Medic.
4. Drive the injured person to the nearest clinic, or hospital according to the Rig’s location

## 8. Confined Space Entry

There are two types of Confined Spaces: Permit-Required Confined Spaces and Non-Permit Required Confined Spaces.
blocks.heading_path: Confined Space Entry Procedure > 8. Confined Space Entry

blocks.text: H&P Employees ARE NOT allowed to enter Permit-Required Confined Spaces. They are only allowed to enter Non-Permit Required Confined Spaces. A space must be evaluated, using H&P’s Confined Space permitting process before entry to determine if it is permit-required or non-permit required. If any danger is detected, the space is classified as a Permit-Required Confined Space, and it WILL NOT BE ENTERED.

## 5. PROCEDURE

### 5.1.GENERAL

Every Confined Space shall be preliminary identified and signalised to avoid entry of non-authorised personnel.

All work involving entry into vessels or confined spaces shall be carried out under the control of a Work Permit, Entry, and Isolation Certificates, in accordance with the Work Permit System.

To meet this requirement, a work permit will be raised to carry out the work within the relevant vessel or space. An Entry certificate will be issued to enable entry into that vessel or space (refer Work Permit System Procedure K-KS-HS-PR-008).

### EMERGENCY TEAM LEADER:

The Emergency Team Leader for the shift should be invited to attend the TRIC job planning process. While in attendance he will be able to view the confined space site, access to the site, and review the rescue plan developed for the specific job being done. SEE APPENDIX 4.1 (CSE Rescue plan sample)

Confined spaces are defined as a tank, mud pit, and tunnel or similar where there is a danger of explosion, lack of oxygen or the presence of toxic gases. All spaces that are not normally lit, not normally ventilated and not normally manned or designed to be manned are also considered confined spaces, as are spaces with difficult or restricted access.

A permit to work must be completed for any work involving confined space or tank entry and a copy must be posted at the confined space entry display board outside the area to be entered. (See Appendix 4.2)

**8.5.** Authorized Entrants duties include:

- a. Understanding potential hazards, warning signs, modes of transmission, and consequences of exposure to the potential hazards in the confined space.
- b. Reviewing applicable safety data sheets prior to start of work.
- c. Verify test results are recorded on the Entry permit and initialed by the person performing the atmospheric test.
- d. Proper use of equipment for ventilation, communication, personnel protection, lighting, entry and exits for carrying out emergency rescue from the confined space.
- e. Wear full body harness attached to mechanical means of retrieval for non-entry rescue.
- f. Maintain communication with the Attendant at all times.
- g. Notify the Attendant when evacuation is necessary.
- h. Initiate SELF-RESCUE when the Attendant orders an evacuation, the evacuation alarm sounds or a prohibited condition and/or danger is perceived.

### ENTRANT:

- Have thorough knowledge of and understand this procedure.
- Participate in the job planning process including TRIC & TBT.
- Be aware of the hazards that exist in the space and understand the requirements of the ERP.
- Understand the controls in place and follow the permit conditions.
- Be ready to evacuate quickly and without assistance if possible.
- Be alert to signs of exposure or heat stress.
- Must be trained & competent to use any specialized PPE required for the task.
- Inspect, test and make proper use of equipment and protective devices
- Maintain communication with the standby man to enable him to monitor the entrants’ status.
- Alert the standby man if an unsafe condition arises or when symptoms of exposure appear.
- Stop the work and come out from the confined space as soon as possible when:
  - Ordered by the standby man
  - He recognises the warning signs or symptoms of exposure.
  - An unsafe condition arises.
  - An emergency is sounded

### 5.12. ENTRY REQUIREMENTS

The person who authorises the confined space entry must ensure the following requirements:

- Atmospheric Test has been performed.
- The Work Permit and Confined Space Entry Certificate were correctly issued.
- Personnel involved in the confined space entry are trained.
- All pre-entry requirements as outlined on the permit have been completed before any worker is allowed to enter the confined space.
- All required pre-entry conditions have been met.
- The standby person is outside of the confined space ready to give assistance in case of emergency.
- Gas bottles, other than breathing apparatus sets, shall never be taken into a confined space.
- The emergency rescue team are informed about the activity and available.
- Communication channels are established and any communication equipment which would be used to summon either the rescue team or other emergency assistance is operating correctly.
- The emergency arrangements (Rescue Tripod) should be available and ready to use.
- Terminate the entry upon becoming aware of a condition or set of conditions whose hazard potential exceeds the limits authorised by the entry permit.

blocks.text:
**8.7.** Authorized Attendant will be named on the Entry Permit. His duties include:

- a. Always remain outside the confined space during entry operations unless relieved by another authorized and trained Attendant.
- b. An accurate count and identification of all individuals in the confined space shall be maintained at all times.
- c. Understand and recognize potential confined space exposures.
- d. Monitor activities and conditions internal and external to the confined space to determine if it is safe for entrant to remain in the space.
- e. Maintain effective and continuous contact with the entrants.
- f. Evacuate entrants immediately when:
  - i. A condition is observed which is not allowed in the Confined Space Entry Permit
  - ii. Entrants’ behavior reflects an adverse reaction to a suspected hazardous condition with-in the Confined Space
  - iii. A hazard is detected, either inside or outside the Confined Space that could endanger Entrants.
- g. Summon rescue and other emergency services when it is determined that Entrants may need assistance to escape from the Confined Space. Do Not Attempt To Enter The Confined Space.
- h. Perform Non-Entry rescue if possible using a mechanical lifting device and a retrieval line connected to the Entrants’ full body harness.
- i. Perform No Other Duties that might interfere with the primary responsibility of monitoring and protecting Entrants.

### 4.7.STANDBY MAN

The Workgroup Leader shall nominate one of his workgroup members to fulfil the role as a Standby Man and brief him fully on his duties.

The Standby Man shall have no responsibilities other than to continually watch those inside the confined space and observe their condition and be alert to any need for rescue or other assistance by those inside.

## 4. ROLES AND RESPONSIBILITIES

### 4.7.STANDBY MAN

While acting as Standby Man for entry confined space, these following must always be followed:

- In case of emergency, he must not enter the confined space, but raise alarm or initiate the emergency procedures.
- Maintain a confined space entry log and a continuous count of entrants using form K-KS-HS-FO-057.
- Maintain such position as to physically observe the condition of every person inside the confined space.
- Install a barrier on the entry to avoid any unauthorized entry inside the confined space.
- Carry routine checks of equipment being used to continually monitor the atmosphere.
- Operate and maintain the Emergency Rescue Tripod in ready condition for rescue any personnel from inside the confined space.
- Maintain continuous communication with all workers within the confined space. This may be by voice, watching them or any other equally effective means. If it is not possible to maintain communication with a worker because of the actual location in the space, arrangements must be made so that Standby Man is continuously aware of that worker's location and condition.
- Be aware of the nearest telephone and establish any required communication (updates / emergency conditions) with relevant workgroup leaders or supervisors.
- Have adequate personal protective equipment available, so if it should become necessary to aid those inside the confined space, the rescue team can enter the area safely after the notification of Toolpusher / Safety officer.
- In the event of a rig alarm or any emergency, instruct the personnel within the confined space to evacuate immediately and proceed to muster point and inform the muster checker when the confined space is clear of personnel.
- Never under any circumstances enter the confined space whilst fulfilling the role as a standby man.

### STAND-BY WATCHMAN:

A standby man must be assigned and clearly identifiable for any confined space entry. A standby man must have no other duties while so assigned. Only personnel who have satisfactorily completed the KCAD confined space training and properly trained to carry out this task can be authorized by the PIC to be assigned as a stand-by person. He must attend and participate in toolbox talk prior to the start of work. He must remain outside the confined space, in a safe atmosphere and safe physical position, at all times during a confined space entry operation and perform the assigned duties under this procedure.

The stand-by person is responsible for carrying out the following duties:

- Assist the competent person to sample the atmosphere for oxygen and combustible or toxic gas levels.
- Ensure adequate rescue equipment is readily available and familiarize themselves with its operation and what to do in the event of an emergency.
- Verifies conditions and sign the permit to work before beginning work.
- Remain at the confined space entry site while any personnel are inside.
- Prevent unauthorized people from entering the confined space.
- Keep the display board updated maintaining a tally of the persons inside the confined space.
- Marking airlines and / or safety lines so that each individual inside the tank is clearly identified in the event of a problem
- A standby man will have no other duties while so assigned.
- Standby man must understand the requirements of the ERP.
- Continually monitor the confined space entrance and adjacent areas for hazards that may affect the personnel working in the confined space.
- Maintain contact with a manned control point (if available) and with the personnel in the confined space.
- Notify the person in charge of carrying out the work of their intention to leave the work area, so that a handover of responsibility to a suitable replacement can be carried out.
- Immediately raising the alarm if there are indications (via the agreed system of communication ie radio, telephone or otherwise), of the personnel within the confined space being affected by the atmosphere or any other form of hazard to summon assistance rapidly if entrants get into trouble.

### 3.5 TOOLBOX TALK (TRIC)

Before starting the job, Toolbox Talk (TRIC) will be held “at the job site” by the person in charge and include all personnel involved in the planned task. It is important that crew names are entered onto the TRIC card to ensure accountability for “who is going to do what”.

If the job changes or a new person comes into the team, a Time Out for Safety (TOFS) must be taken to control the change. In the case of a new person joining the team, that person shall be briefed about all identified hazards, the corresponding control measures and any responsibilities expected of him. The TRIC card must then be reviewed and amended to control any risk associated with the change in the job or the new person joining the activity. Every member of the work party shall sign on the TBT to ensure that he has understood the hazards, controls and the ERP.

## 9. Confined Space Exit

The exit from Confined Space and the returning of the area back to service will follow a systematic approach. The Authorized Attendant confirms that all personnel have exited the Confined Space and Entry Supervisor (Local Area Authority) notes any problems and closes Entry Permit. All hole covers and/or hatches will be re-installed immediately after completing the task.

blocks.text:
## 10. Training

H&P will provide training so that employees who work in Confined Spaces as Authorized Entrants, outside as Authorized Attendants or as Entry Supervisors (Local Area Authority), acquire the understanding, knowledge, and skills necessary to prevent injury or loss of life.
blocks.heading_path: Confined Space Entry Procedure > 10. Training

blocks.text:
**10.1.** Training will be provided to each affected employee:

- a. Before the employee is involved in a Confined Space Entry
- b. Before there is a change in assigned duties
- c. Whenever there is a change in Confined Space Operations that presents a hazard about which the employees have not been previously trained
- d. Refresher training will be conducted annually and whenever the employer has reason to believe that there are either deviations from the Confined Space Entry Procedure or that there are inadequacies in the employee’s knowledge or use of these procedures.

**10.2.** Training will establish employee proficiency in the duties as authorized entrant, attendant, or entry supervisor (Local Area Authority). H&P will introduce new or revised procedures as necessary.

**10.3.** H&P will document that training as been completed.
blocks.heading_path: Confined Space Entry Procedure > 10. Training > 10.1-10.3

blocks.text:
### 3.3 TRAINING

Any personnel entering a confined space must be trained in the hazards of confined space entry and use of the equipment, including SCBA that must be utilized. This training and practical demonstration must be given in a formal manner and fully documented. Responsible persons and the Emergency Response Teams (ERT) must be trained and exercised in the proper use of the installation or facility-specific confined space rescue and retrieval equipment and the training and drills must be documented.

**Who needs training?**

- PIC
- Permit requestor
- Entrant
- Risk assessment team
- Standby man
- Emergency response team
- Authorized gas tester

## 11. 3rd-party Contractor Requirements

When a 3rd-party contractor will be performing work in an H&P owned Confined Space, the following steps will be completed by H&Ps Entry Supervisor (Local Area Authority).
blocks.heading_path: Confined Space Entry Procedure > 11. 3rd-party Contractor Requirements

blocks.text:
**11.1.** Inform 3rd-party contractors that the work being performed will be conducted in a confined space.

**11.2.** Inform 3rd-party contractors why the space is designated as a confined space.

**11.3.** Inform 3rd-party contractors of past experiences and hazards associated with any confined spaces.

**11.4.** Inform 3rd-party contractors of any precautions, procedures and emergency response plans that have been implemented to protect all persons in the confined space.

**11.5.** If both H&P and Operator’s 3rd-party employees will be making entries, the entries will be coordinated between the H&P entry supervisor (Local Area Authority) and the Operator’s supervisor.

**11.6.** H&P’s entry supervisor (Local Area Authority) will confirm that confined space hazards and permit procedure training has been provided by the 3rd-party employer called out by H&P.

**11.7.** H&Ps entry supervisor (Local Area Authority) will debrief the 3rd-party employee(s) at the conclusion of the entry regarding the procedure used for entry and any hazards encountered or created in the Confined Space. Hazards will be noted on the Permit for future reference.

## 2. Definitions

**2.1.** Confined Space – a space on the rig or yard that:

- a. Has limited openings for entry and/or exit, including difficult or restricted access
- b. May contain known or potential hazards, including the danger of explosion, lack of oxygen, or the presence of toxic gases
- c. Is not intended for continuous occupancy and is not normally manned or designed to be manned
- d. Has insufficient natural ventilation, is not normally ventilated, and may not be normally lit

*Examples of confined spaces in H&P Drilling Operations include but are not limited to tanks, mud pits, tunnels, and similar spaces meeting the criteria above.*

Confined Space Entry Procedure > 3. Proc.text:
## 3. Process

**3.1.** Atmospheric testing shall be conducted prior to and continuously during confined space entry. Toxic gases may not be detectable until sludge or scale is disturbed in the confined space. In Oman operations, the person performing pre-entry gas testing and/or air quality sampling shall be an Authorized Gas Tester who has undergone applicable training in the gas testing process and understands the function and limitations of the test equipment in use.

The Authorized Gas Tester shall use correctly calibrated gas-testing equipment to verify a safe atmosphere by confirming that oxygen, flammable, and toxic levels are within acceptable limits and that the space is free of toxic and flammable gases. For reference in interpreting atmospheric conditions, normal air contains approximately nitrogen 78.09%, oxygen 20.95%, carbon dioxide 0.04%, and other gases 0.92%.

The initial results of testing shall be recorded on the electronic or written permit; in Oman operations, the air-quality test results shall be entered on the PTW/CSE permit issued for the confined space entry job. If during continuous monitoring conditions become immediately dangerous, the job shall be stopped, the permit shall be closed, and the supervisor notified. If the confined space is left for any reason, the atmosphere shall be re-tested before re-entry is permitted, and the atmosphere shall also be re-tested after any emergency case or situation.

If the confined space is left for any reason, the atmosphere shall be re-tested before re-entry is permitted, and the atmosphere shall also be re-tested after any emergency case or situation.
